text: You know, movie adaptations of popular
video game franchises are nothing new.
But apart from Resident Evil, which is
basically a case study in Hollywood
insanity, the most frustrating of them
all has got to be Tomb Raider. I mean,
Lara Croft as a character was
practically tailor made for the big
screen. She's a modern-day Indiana Jones
with a touch of James Bond upper class
British sophistication and a fantastic
pair of uh eyes. She goes on globe
trottting adventures to find lost tombs
and recover ancient artifacts, dodging
traps, dangerous wildlife, and rival
tomb raiders that are hot on her trail.
It's not exactly rocket science, but for
some reason, Hollywood just can't seem
to nail it. In fact, the more times they
try, the worse they seem to fail. They
started out pretty strong, at least. The
Angelina Jolie movie from 2001 was a
decent stab at adapting Lara Croft to
the big screen, even if the plot was a
bit all over the place. The action
scenes were fun and suitably
over-the-top. The tombs looked like
actual tombs from the games. Jolie was
about as close as any actress could get
to Lara, and there was a genuine sense
of fun and adventure to the whole thing.
It's also kind of funny to see a
pre-bonded Daniel Craig trying to make
the most of a pretty thankless role. The
movie took itself just seriously enough
to get you invested without getting
bogged down in the ethics of stealing
historical artifacts from other
cultures. [ __ ] it. If they really cared
that much about them, they wouldn't have
left them buried in the ground for a
thousand years. Anyway, I digress.
Angelina Jolie clearly understood the
assignment on this one. She got into
surprisingly good shape for the role and
was happy to play up to Lara's fem fatal
side. I mean, middle-aged Angelina
probably considers it a horribly dated
piece of exploitation from a less
enlightened time, but I'm pretty sure
she was happy enough to take the
paycheck back in the day. Funny how that
works, isn't it? The movie went down
pretty well with fans and made enough
money to greenlight a sequel, The Cradle
of Life, which squandered all that
goodwill on a meandering plot, a shitty
and forgettable villain, and action
scenes that felt like a distinct step
down from the previous movie.
Apparently, director Yan Deont hated
working on this film and felt like he
was being pulled in different directions
by too many different people. These old
Tomb Raider movies seem to have a weird
knack for casting actors just before
they hit the big time, though. In this
case, a very young and fresh-faced
looking Gerrard Butler not long before
he was cast in 300s. Just like Daniel
Craig before him, you can tell he's
doing his best to get noticed in a role
where basically he's just a sidekick.
Cradle of Life made over a hundred
million less than the first film. And
although the studio were up for doing a
third movie, Angelina Jolie decided to
move on to bigger and better things and
refused to come back. Can't say I blame
her really. As if this wasn't bad
enough, the games were also going
through a lull in popularity, struggling
to make the transition to the next
generation of games consoles. Lara Croft
just wasn't the cool, sexy icon that
she'd been a few years earlier, and so
interesting movies faded away. It would
be another 15 years before they'd even
try again. The games had been rebooted
for a new generation. Lara Croft had
been reimagined from a fearless
adventurer motivated by challenge and
personal gain to a much more fragile and
vulnerable character, always on the back
foot, struggling just to stay alive,
always apologizing for everything and
usually beat up and dirty and terrified.
Oh yeah, and crying. Lots of [ __ ]
crying. Anyway, somehow it worked. The
games were a big success and so it
wasn't long before Hollywood decided to
take another crack at Lara Croft. The
result was Tomb Raider 2018, a movie
that's basically the personification of
meh. Too cluttered, too serious, and too
grounded to be much fun. The action was
meh. The villains were meh. And worst of
all, Alicia Vicander's performance as
Lara was meh. And that really was the
biggest problem with this film, its
star. The games were smart enough to put
Lara front and center because the
designers knew that Lara was the thing
gamers would resonate with most. And I
don't just mean sweaty 13-year-old boys
that had just discovered they really
like boobs. I mean that just like James
Bond and Indiana Jones before her, the
character herself became bigger and more
memorable than the adventures she got
involved in. For all their faults, the
previous movies understood this too,
which was why they were called Lara
Croft Tomb Raider and cast an actress
with actual charisma and screen presence
to play the leads. To be fair to her,
Vikander definitely got in great shape
for the role, and she looks like she
could actually do some of the stuff her
character does, but there's just
something missing from her performance.
And no, I'm not just talking about the
obvious. You dirty fcker. Instead of
being this larger than-l life fem fatal
adventurer who's beautiful and
mysterious and deadly and not afraid to
make use of her own sensuality from time
to time, she's just a generic action
hero in a tank top. There's nothing
unique or compelling about her. nothing
that really marks her out as Lara Croft.
She could be anyone really. And because
of that, she kind of fades into the
background, swallowed up and overwhelmed
by all the action and scenery and other
actors. I don't know, man. Nobody seemed
to be having a great time with this one,
and I think audiences at the time could
feel it. The film got lukewarm reviews.
The studio couldn't seem to decide on a
way forward, and ultimately, a planned
sequel was abandoned when Amazon bought
up the rights to the series. But don't
worry, dear viewer, we'll get to that
one in a moment. Before that, there was
the Tomb Raider TV series on Netflix,
which was apparently set in the same
continuity as the rebooted video games,
but I don't really give a [ __ ] because
it was so god-awful. Lara Croft now
combines the worst aspects of the
rebooted character, constantly moping
around, obsessing over her daddy issues
and feeling guilty just for existing
with a character design that looks like
it sprang from the fever dreams of Neil
Duckman. Why the [ __ ] does she have arms
and shoulders and a jawline bigger than
most men? Why do modern artists hate the
very idea of femininity? Oh yeah, and
she's gay now. Because of course she is.
Because the idea of platonic female
relationships are to modern writers what
democratic election results are to Mark
Hamill. Needless to say, the show was a
[ __ ] disaster. Soaked in identity
politics and completely devoid of fun
and adventure. But the worst was yet to
come because we were just about to enter
into the Phoebe Waller Bridge
era.
A see up until they fired her for gross
incompetence, Amazon Studios were run by
Jennifer Sulki, a woman I could best
describe as Amazon's answer to Kathleen
Kennedy. Honestly, middle-aged liberal
white women are to fun and entertainment
what Chuck- E-Cheese is to healthy
eating. She wasn't great at running a
studio and famously [ __ ] canned a Conan
the Barbarian series because it had too
much toxic masculinity in it.
But one thing old Jen loved most of all
were girl bosses. And since Amazon now
had the rights to Tomb Raider, she was
keen to put even more of a feminist
stamp on it. So she hired British writer
Phoebe Waller Bridge as part of a $20
million development contract to come up
with a new Tomb Raider TV show for
Amazon Prime.
Really? I don't know how she managed it,
but Phoebe Waller Bridge has somehow
spent almost a decade coasting on the
success of one good TV show. Hasn't
achieved anything of value since then.
But somehow Hollywood still treats her
like she's this literary messiah that's
going to transform the entire industry.
And even if she was actually capable of
seeing projects through to completion,
[ __ ] only knows why you'd choose the
writer of Flea Bag, a comedy drama about
dysfunctional relationships to helm your
big budget action adventure series. I
mean, Phoebe claims to be a big fan of
the games, but then again, Leslie
Heddlin claimed to be a big fan of Star
Wars, so make of that what you will.
Well, that's definitely
shite. I've seen the script polish that
she inflicted on No Time To Die. And I
can tell you right now, her creative
sensibilities are as far removed from
Tomb Raider as Bella Ramsay is from good
acting. As if all that wasn't bad
enough, they also had their chosen
actress for Lara Croft's. I have a bad
very bad bad bad feeling about this.
Remember how I just got done talking
about charisma and magnetism and
personality being a huge part of Lara's
appeal? Well, how about casting an
actress who never once demonstrated any
of those
things. Honestly, after the absolute
embarrassment of Dark Phoenix, I figured
that Hollywood would stop trying to make
Sophie Turner happen because clearly
she's not going to happen. But
apparently they're going to give it one
more shot with Tomb Raider. It's a bold
strategy, Cotton. Let's see if it pays
off for him. Honestly, an actress who
can't act and never once showed any
inclination towards action movies
playing Lara Croft, written by a creator
who's never done anything like this
before and hasn't achieved anything
meaningful in almost 10 years. What
could possibly go wrong? Apparently
quite a lot. Because this show has
fluctuated between cancelled and active
for at least a year now. There's been no
word of any real progress on it for even
longer. And with Jennifer Sulki ousted
as head of Amazon Studios, it kind of
makes you wonder if it's ever going to
see the light of day. I mean,
personally, I hope it does just for the
sheer hilarity it's going to bring. My
point though is that Lara Croft is like
some beautiful old Victorian house at
this point, remodeled and redecorated as
the fads and fashions of each new decade
came and went, every new generation
adding more [ __ ] to something that was
never designed to accommodate it in the
first place. The fact is, Hollywood
can't get Lara Croft right because she's
the product of a very different era
that's fundamentally incompatible with
how stories are written now. And every
time they try to adapt her for a modern
audio, all they really do is expose how
laughably [ __ ] their writing's become
now. So, good luck, Phoebe, and the fine
people at Amazon Studios. I'll be
looking forward to seeing what you
produce.
Anyway, that's all I've got for
today. Go away now.
